LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFFS EDWARD ARNOLD AND JEANNE ARNOLD'S MOTION FOR SANCTIONS [CCP 128.7] IS DENIED. ALTHOUGH DEFENDANTS' OPPOSITION TO THE MOTION TO DISQUALIFY WAS UNSUCCESSFUL, IT WAS NOT FRIVOLOUS OR SO WITHOUT SUPPORT THAT IT MERITS AN AWARD OF SANCTIONS UNDER SECTION 128.7. THIS RULING WAS ISSUED BY JUDGE PETER BUSCH BUT CONFIRMED THIS MORNING BY JUDGE MARLA J. MILLER. JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER; CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, COURT REPORTER: LAURA MARTINEZ, CSR #11332

Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Disqualification Of Def'S Counsel And Mtn To Strike Def'S Mtn For Sanctions
Off Calendar Aug-30-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFFS EDWARD ARNOLD AND JEANNE ARNOLD'S MOTION FOR DISQUALIFICATION OF DEFENDANT'S COUNSEL AND MOTION TO STRIKE DEFENDANT'S MOTION FOR SANCTIONS

Appearances:
Jeven Sloan of Loewinsohn Flegle Deary LLP (205.314.0627) for plaintiffs and moving parties; Stephen H. Dye of Schnader Harrison Segal & Lewis LLP (415.364.6713) for defendants and responding parties.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: the motion to disqualify is granted. Mr. Trujillo, acting as plaintiff's local counsel, was a significant participant in the Pennsylvania action and acquired confidential information in that role that is directly relevant to the California action. For purposes of the disqualification analysis, the two actions are one and the same, being based on exactly the same facts and claims, and the California action being the continuation of the Pennsylvania one in a proper forum. Therefore, he was actually involved in the representation of his former client before he "switched sides" to join the firm representing defendant. In the jargon of the disqualification cases, Mr. Trujillo is the "tainted" attorney. Mr. Trujillo was still plaintiffs' counsel when he began negotiations with his current firm. Neither he nor his current firm advised plaintiff of his impending switch in sides until after the negotiations were complete. It was only then that any effort to set up an ethical screen took place. Although the Court has no reason to believe that Mr. Trujillo had any communications with the attorneys actually handling this case at his new firm or that he disclosed any confidential information to anyone, he has contributed a declaration to his new firm's effort to defeat this motion. Arguably, in light of Mr. Trujillo's actual, substantial representation of his first clients before switching sides to join plaintiffs' adversary, this remains a situation where disqualification is compelled even under Kirk v. First American Title Insurance (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 776 at 815. Even assuming, however, that the Court retains discretion to consider whether the ethical screen rebuts the presumption of vicarious disqualification, the Court would conclude that this is not an appropriate case to permit the ethical screen to do so given all the facts and circumstances and considering all the factors to be balanced under Kirk. Although the attorney actually representing defendant is in an office geographically removed from the office where Mr. Trujillo works, although the ethical screen eventually set up may be sufficient to avoid disclosure of confidences, and although defendant, also an innocent party, may well experience some dislocation and additional expense, the integrity of the judicial system, the need for client confidence in the loyalty of attorneys, and the very direct and substantial involvement of the tainted attorney in the on-going litigation compel disqualification. The Court notes that the burden on defendant may be relatively less than in many cases given that the only remaining matter pending at the trial court level is a motion for sanctions (a generally disfavored motion) that has been fully briefed, and it is not uncommon to engage new appellate counsel (should an appeal eventually be filed in this case). The motion to strike the sanctions motion is denied. That relief is unnecessary. The motion will go off calendar subject to defendant's new counsel re-noticing it should new counsel decide to do so. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Peter J. Busch, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Tamra Keen, CSR #5404 (Tel: 925-640-4599, Email: TKeenCSR@gmail.com)

Defendants Kam & Reinemann, Inc. And Steven Kam'S Motion For Sanctions

MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS KAM & REINEMANN, INC AND STEVEN KAM'S MOTION FOR SANCTIONS.

No appearances. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Having read and considered courtesy copies of Plaintiffs' Motion for Disqualification (the memorandum of points and authorities for which appears to violate the length and format requirements of California Rule of Court 3.1113(d) and (f)) and Defendants' Opposition to the ex parte request to continue, the Court, on its own motion, orders the hearing on the motion for sanctions currently set for August 9, 2013, to be continued until after the motion to disqualify has been determined. Absent further order of the Court, the hearing on the sanctions motion is continued to Friday, August 30, 2013, in Department 504 at 2:30 p.m. The Court concludes that it is not appropriate to hear argument on the merits of the sanctions motion while there is a pending issue as to whether Defendants' counsel presenting that argument is permitted to continue representing his clients under governing ethical principles. Judge: Peter J. Busch, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (504)

Off Calendar May-03-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS KAM & REINEMANN, INC. AND STEVEN KAM'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF ISSUES

Appearances: Stephen H. Dye of Schnader Harrison Segal & Lewis LLP (415.364.6713) for defendants and moving parties;
Jeven Sloan of Loewinsohn Flegle Deary LLP (205.314.0627) for plaintiffs and responding parties.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiffs suffered cognizable injury and the statute of limitations began to run no later than December 21, 2006 when the IRS had concluded its audit process and issued its FPAA. The statute was not tolled by subsequent efforts to mitigate or reverse that decision. Therefore, applying the three-year statute of limitations for which Plaintiffs advocate, the statute had run long prior to this case being filed. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order in compliance with CCP 437 c(g) and CRC 3.1312. Judge: Peter J. Busch, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Brenda L. Crow, CSR #10503 (916.872.7488, BLeeCrow@gmail.com)
